appeal_letter:
Re: Appeal of denial of Inpatient Detoxification (05/18/2026 to 05/21/2026)

Dear Appeals Reviewer,

I am appealing the denial of the requested Inpatient Detoxification admission for the dates of service 05/18/2026 through 05/21/2026.

The denial states that, under Aetna's Clinical Policy Bulletins and the ASAM Criteria, the clinical information provided does not demonstrate severe or complicated withdrawal symptoms (such as Delirium Tremens or seizures) requiring 24-hour medical nursing and monitoring, that vital signs are stable and there are no severe co-occurring medical conditions, and that care can therefore be delivered safely at a lower level of care such as an ambulatory (outpatient) detox program. The denial concluded that the requested inpatient service is not medically necessary.

I ask the reviewer to apply that same medical-necessity standard to the documented clinical facts of this case, which I believe support inpatient detoxification rather than outpatient care. The record reflects a 10-year history of heavy daily alcohol use. On a recent attempt to stop drinking without medical supervision, I experienced severe tremors, profuse sweating, and visual hallucinations, and was seen in the emergency room. My provider documented a CIWA score of 18, which is in the moderate-to-severe range, and noted a high risk of progression to seizures based on my prior withdrawal history.

I want to address the denial's central point directly. The denial relies on the absence of Delirium Tremens or active seizures and on currently stable vital signs. The documented facts show withdrawal that is already moderate-to-severe rather than mild: visual hallucinations are a perceptual disturbance of the kind that distinguishes complicated withdrawal from an uncomplicated course, and the provider has identified a specific, individualized high risk of progression to seizures grounded in my own prior withdrawal history. The clinical concern is precisely that an outpatient setting cannot deliver the 24-hour monitoring needed to detect and intervene before that progression occurs. Stable vital signs at one point in time do not establish that the withdrawal will remain stable, particularly where a documented prior history points to escalation. Applied to these individualized facts, the medical-necessity criteria support 24-hour medically supervised inpatient detoxification.

If the reviewer concludes that any specific threshold in the cited criteria is not literally met on the present record, I ask that the individualized clinical facts above, especially the documented hallucinations, the CIWA score of 18, and the provider-noted high risk of seizure progression from prior withdrawals, be weighed as the basis for an exception to a lower level of care.

I also note a procedural point. The denial advised of the right to appeal within 180 days, but it did not describe the process for submitting an appeal or confirm the member's right to a full and fair review of this decision. I request a complete and fair review and ask that the appeal procedure be provided to me.

I request that the denial be overturned and that the Inpatient Detoxification admission for 05/18/2026 through 05/21/2026 be authorized as medically necessary. I am happy to supply any additional records the reviewer needs.

Not legal or medical advice. Draft assistance only.

Respectfully,
Member
missing_evidence_checklist: Full ER and provider records documenting the CIWA score of 18, the hallucinations, tremors, and sweating, and the provider's note on seizure-progression risk
Documentation of the prior withdrawal history relied on to support high seizure risk
The treating provider's statement or letter of medical necessity explaining why outpatient detox is unsafe in this case
Any serial vital-sign and CIWA reassessments showing the trajectory of withdrawal over time
Aetna's appeal submission instructions, forms, and deadlines, and confirmation of the right to a full and fair review
The specific Clinical Policy Bulletin and ASAM level-of-care criteria text the denial relied on, to compare against the documented facts
